coredata.dc:title: Lignin/carbon dots-embedded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel: An effective, inexpensive and versatile platform for doxycycline adsorption and determination

coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel hydrogel, lignin/carbon dots-embedded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel (LCCH), was fabricated for doxycycline (DOX) adsorption and determination. The adsorption characteristics of LCCH under various conditions were investigated. According to the Langmuir model fitting results, the maximum capacity of LCCH reached 1686 mg/g. Moreover, it exhibited excellent reusability, with an adsorption capacity maintained above 80.4 % after 5 cycles. Additionally, the LCCH demonstrated stable fluorescence properties, with DOX causing significant quenching of fluorescence attributed to both the internal filtration effect and static quenching. Within the ranges of 10–120 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L, the fluorescence of LCCH gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of DOX, reaching a limit of detection as low as 0.08 μg/L. Lastly, a cost analysis considering energy consumption and reagent prices demonstrated LCCH's cost-effectiveness as an adsorbent. Overall, this study presents a novel approach for designing and preparing an effective, inexpensive, and versatile hydrogel for the removal of antibiotics.

head.ce:title: Lignin/carbon dots-embedded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel: An effective, inexpensive and versatile platform for doxycycline adsorption and determination

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel hydrogel, lignin/carbon dots-embedded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel (LCCH), was fabricated for doxycycline (DOX) adsorption and determination. The adsorption characteristics of LCCH under various conditions were investigated. According to the Langmuir model fitting results, the maximum capacity of LCCH reached 1686 mg/g. Moreover, it exhibited excellent reusability, with an adsorption capacity maintained above 80.4 % after 5 cycles. Additionally, the LCCH demonstrated stable fluorescence properties, with DOX causing significant quenching of fluorescence attributed to both the internal filtration effect and static quenching. Within the ranges of 10–120 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L, the fluorescence of LCCH gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of DOX, reaching a limit of detection as low as 0.08 μg/L. Lastly, a cost analysis considering energy consumption and reagent prices demonstrated LCCH's cost-effectiveness as an adsorbent. Overall, this study presents a novel approach for designing and preparing an effective, inexpensive, and versatile hydrogel for the removal of antibiotics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline (DOX) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic commonly used to treat various bacterial infections, including respiratory tract infections, urinary tract infections, skin infections, and specific sexually transmitted diseases like chlamydia and gonorrhea (Chen et al., 2024). DOX enters the environment through multiple pathways, such as human and animal excretions, improper disposal, and wastewater from pharmaceutical manufacturing processes (Gaballah et al., 2021). Once released into ecosystems, it can accumulate in water bodies, soil, and sediments, posing risks to aquatic organisms, plants, and microbial communities (Scaria et al., 2021). The ecological impacts of DOX pollution include disruptions in food chains, alterations in microbial populations, and potential endangerment of vulnerable species (Zhou et al., 2011). Therefore, the efficient removal of DOX is urgent and highly significant.

Currently, several technologies have been proposed for the removal of antibiotics, including advanced oxidation, photocatalytic degradation, membrane separation, biodegradation, and adsorption (Sharma et al., 2023). Advanced oxidation technology requires specialized equipment and specific chemicals, resulting in high investment and operational maintenance costs (Preethi et al., 2024). Photocatalytic degradation can treat antibiotics; however, its high energy consumption and by-product formation are significant drawbacks. Membrane separation helps remove antibiotics from water but has disadvantages, such as membrane clogging, frequent cleaning requirements, and high investment costs (Osman et al., 2024). Biodegradation's efficiency in removing antibiotics depends highly on the species, abundance, and activity of microorganisms, and its degradation cycle is relatively long (Abdelfattah et al., 2023). Among these technologies, adsorption stands out due to its environmental friendliness, non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and efficiency (Hu et al., 2020). Extensive research has been conducted on the potential of various hydrogels for adsorption. Lignin, an aromatic polymer, holds significant promise as a raw material for preparing functional hydrogels (Ge and Li, 2018). Li et al. (2023) fabricated an organic–inorganic ternary hydrogel composed of lignin, montmorillonite, and cellulose, which was used for the adsorptive removal of ciprofloxacin. Liu et al. (2021) prepared lignin hydrogels loaded with nano-FeS and demonstrated a tetracycline adsorption capacity of 66.49 mg/g. Therefore, lignin-based hydrogels hold considerable potential for antibiotic removal. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNs) are nanoscale fibers derived from cellulose, abundant in nature and obtainable from renewable sources like wood pulp, agricultural residues, or other biomass (Nechyporchuk et al., 2016). CNs possess a high aspect ratio and substantial surface area, offering abundant active sites for adsorption (Lobmann and Svagan, 2017). Hence, CNs are promising candidates for preparing hydrogels to remove antibiotics.

Various methods have played pivotal roles in detecting antibiotics, including high-performance liquid chromatography, immunoassays, colorimetric methods, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Despite their high sensitivity and accuracy, these methods often face challenges due to complex pre-treatment procedures, prolonged assay durations, and operational difficulties (Majdinasab et al., 2020). In response to these limitations, fluorescence determination has gained increasing attention. Carbon dots (CDs), as high-performance carbon nanomaterials, have extensive applications in the fluorescence detection of antibiotics (Li et al., 2022). For instance, Zhang et al. (2024) fabricated CDs that can selectively detect DOX. Feng et al. (2018) developed CDs coated with molecularly imprinted polymers, which can specifically and rapidly recognize DOX. These examples highlight the significant advantages of using CDs for DOX detection. However, the practical application of CDs faces challenges, primarily due to their small interparticle distances, leading to easy aggregation (Gao et al., 2013). To address these issues, loading CDs onto a carrier, especially a lignin-based hydrogel, emerges as a promising solution.

Utilizing lignin, CNs, and CDs as raw materials, we synthesized LCCH for the adsorption and fluorescence detection of DOX. The LCCH was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Adsorption behaviours of LCCH were investigated considering pH, temperature (T), initial concentration (C0), and contact time (t). Additionally, LCCH was utilized for DOX detection based on fluorescence quenching, determining the detection range and lowest detection limit (LOD). The adsorption and detection mechanisms of LCCH for DOX were analyzed, and a cost analysis of LCCH was conducted.

Bleached wood pulp (BWP, 5.0 g) was thoroughly mixed with water (300 mL) through mechanical stirring for 30 min. The main component of the BWP was cellulose. Separately, a solution (designated as solution A) was prepared by adding TEMPO radical (0.5 mmol) and NaBr (5 mmol) to water (200 mL). Subsequently, solution A was slowly dripped into the BWP suspension. Then, 30 mmol of NaClO was added to initiate the reaction. The pH of the suspension was maintained between 10 and 11 throughout the reaction. After five hours, 5 mL of acetone was added to stop the reaction. The oxidation suspensions were then dialyzed against water for five days and homogenized five times using a high-pressure homogenizer to obtain CNs, which were stored at 4 ℃ for subsequent use.

A 10 g solution of FeSO4 (10 wt%) was thoroughly mixed with a 10 g solution of citric acid (10 wt%). Subsequently, 8 mL of ethylenediamine was added dropwise. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling naturally to 25 °C, the solution was filtered, yielding a clarified yellow solution of CDs.

Different volumes of CDs (20 μL, 50 μL, 100 μL, 200 μL, 300 μL, 400 μL, and 500 μL) with a consistent concentration of 0.5 wt% were combined with 10 g of a CNs suspension (1.0 wt%) and homogenized using ultrasonic treatment for 15 min to ensure uniformity. The mixture was then stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 25 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, CDs-CNs-x (where x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7) underwent dialysis against water using a dialysis membrane for 4 days. Finally, appropriate water was added to achieve a concentration of 1.0 wt% for CDs-CNs-x.

An orthogonal array experimental design was employed to analyze four variables: CDs-CNs (factor A), alkali lignin (AL, factor B), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, factor C), and epichlorohydrin (ECH, factor D), each with three levels (Table S1). Subsequently, Following the principle of orthogonality (Jin et al., 2021), an L9 (34) test matrix was constructed to evaluate their influences on the adsorption capacity (Qe) of the prepared hydrogel. The dried weight of the hydrogel was used to calculate the Qe. The optimal preparation parameters for the hydrogel were determined based on the results presented in Table S2.

The preparation process using optimal parameters proceeded as follows: Initially, PVA (0.5 g) was stirred with a sodium hydroxide solution (5 g, 4.0 wt%) at 90 °C, continuously stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 200 rpm for 15 min to obtain a PVA solution (9.0 wt%). Subsequently, 0.1 g of CDs-CNs-3, 1.0 g of an AL solution (1.0 wt%), and 400 μL of ECH were sequentially added. The mixture was stirred evenly and underwent static defoaming for 15 min. It was then transferred into a glass tube and placed in a water bath at 75 °C for 2 h to complete the gelation process, yielding the target product, LCCH-3. LCCH-3 was subsequently washed thoroughly with water three times to remove any unreacted reagents, and then cut into discs with a diameter of 5.0 cm and a thickness of 1.0 cm for further experiments. The real image of the prepared LCCH-3 is shown in Fig. S1, and its density was measured to be 1.02 g/cm3. Hydrogels corresponding to different CDs-CNs-x combinations were designated as LCCH-x (x = 1–7). Additionally, a control lacking CDs was labeled as LCH, while another devoid of both CDs and CNs was labeled as LH.

The batch adsorption of DOX on the prepared hydrogel was investigated, considering parameters such as T, pH, C0, and t. Specifically, 30 mg of the prepared samples were added to 100 mL DOX solutions for adsorption experiments conducted at a stirring speed of 150 rpm. Unless stated otherwise, the C0 was 60 mg/L, pH was maintained at 6, the t was 240 min, and the T was set to 30 °C. After the adsorption process, the DOX solution was filtered, and its concentration was measured at a wavelength of 345 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Batch experiments determined adsorption isotherms at different DOX concentrations (0–4000 mg/L), and provided adsorption kinetic data by varying contact times (0–240 min). The effects of pH were examined across different values (4–8), and temperature effects were assessed at 20–60 °C. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the results represent the mean of at least three independent measurements.

During the cyclic regeneration process, LCCH-3 was easily extracted from the reaction vessel without filtration or centrifugation. LCCH-3 was then immersed in a 0.05 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution for 5 h. Following elution, LCCH-3 was rinsed three times with water. The desorbed LCCH-3 was subsequently used for another adsorption-desorption cycle.

The micromorphology of the fabricated samples was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). The samples for SEM analysis were deposited on conductive adhesive, coated with a thin layer of gold, and observed at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. For chemical functional group analysis, FTIR spectra were recorded using a Bruker VERTEX 70 instrument with sample-KBr pellets, covering the wavenumber range of 4000–600 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. XRD analysis was conducted using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer within the scattering range of 2θ (10–80°) at a speed of 2°/min, employing Cu Kα irradiation, a 40-mA current, and a 40 kV voltage to determine the crystal structures of the samples. PL spectroscopy was performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Technology Co., Japan) covering the wavelength range of 200–730 nm with a resolution of 1.0 nm.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation process of LCCH, which comprises three primary components: lignin, CNs, and CDs. Initially, CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction (Navpreet et al., 2022). Subsequently, CNs were produced using a combination of TEMPO oxidation and high-pressure homogenization. The CDs were then connected to CNs through hydrogen bonding to fabricate CDs-CNs (Chen et al., 2020). Following this, CDs-CNs, AL, and PVA were homogeneously blended. Finally, the introduction of ECH facilitated the cross-linking among CDs-CNs, AL, and PVA (Wu et al., 2019), resulting in the synthesis of LCCH.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the CNs exhibited a smooth fibrillar structure (Qing et al., 2013). TEM imaging (Fig. S2) revealed an approximately uniform distribution of circular CDs. The average particle size of the CDs was approximately 2.8 nm, with a range of 1.0–3.0 nm (Cao et al., 2022). Compared to CNs, CDs-CNs appeared rougher (Fig. S3), likely due to the incorporation of CDs onto the CNs. SEM observations of LCCH-3 (Fig. 2b) depicted a 3D network structure with irregular pore channels, facilitating the adsorption of DOX (Sun et al., 2023).

Fig. 2c shows the FTIR spectra of AL, CNs, CDs-CNs, and LCCH-3. AL exhibited a peak at 1420 cm−1, indicating the C–H vibration of the methyl group (Yuan et al., 2021). For CNs and CDs-CNs, a prominent peak at 1053 cm−1 resulted from C−O−C stretching vibrations, signifying the retention of cellulose's characteristic absorption peak in both materials (Mandal and Chakrabarty, 2011). CDs-CNs displayed two new peaks at 1572 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, attributed to –NH– stretching vibrations, suggesting the connection between CDs and CNs via hydrogen bonding (Navpreet et al., 2022). In the spectrum of LCCH-3, the intensities of peaks from AL and CDs-CNs were reduced, possibly due to their involvements in cross-linking reactions among CDs-CNs, AL, and PVA (Wu et al., 2019).

The XRD patterns of AL, CNs, CDs-CNs, and LCCH-3 are shown in Fig. 2d. AL existed a prominent, broad amorphous halo peak ranging from 14.0° to 28.0° and two crystalline peaks at 2θ = 34.7° and 41.6° (Kumar and Kumar, 2023). Both CNs and CDs-CNs displayed diffraction peaks at 2θ = 17.5° and 21.9°, corresponding to the (110) and (200) planes, respectively, as identified by the JCPDS card reference (JCPDS card no. 00–050–2241) (Chen et al., 2017). CDs-CNs showed two additional peaks at 22.4°, and 29.7° compared to CNs, attributed to the incorporation of CDs (Navpreet et al., 2022). In the XRD patterns of LCCH-3, the aforementioned peaks of AL and CDs-CNs nearly disappeared, likely due to the cross-linking of AL and CDs-CNs with PVA through ECH (Zhou et al., 2022). This cross-linking reaction formed the 3D porous structure of LCCH-3.

Fig. 2e presents the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of LCCH-3. The UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed peaks at 280 nm and 345 nm, corresponding to π→π* electronic transitions in the indoline part and n→π* transitions at the carbon lattice edge, respectively (Navpreet et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2023). In the PL spectrum of LCCH-3, the excitation wavelength was 377 nm, corresponding to an emission peak at 475 nm. As shown in Fig. 2f, the PL emissions were measured at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 362 to 392 nm, with a slight shift in the fluorescence emission peak to longer wavelengths observed. The excitation-dependent emission nature of LCCH-3 were due to the unique properties of CDs (Zhu et al., 2013).

Fig. 2g illustrates the color changes of LCCH-x under visible and UV light at 365 nm. These color variations likely resulted from differences in the content of CDs. Notably, among the LCCH samples, LCCH-3 exhibited the brightest fluorescence. Furthermore, the absolute quantum yield (AQY0) of LCCH-3 was calculated to be 10.7 %. The photostability of LCCH-3 and CDs was measured at 360 nm excitation over various time periods, as presented in Fig. S4. The results demonstrated that LCCH-3 maintained greater stability after prolonged excitation.

The optimization of DOX adsorption was conducted using response surface methodology (Sharma et al., 2023). Three crucial factors—pH, T, and C0—were systematically considered, as detailed in Table S3. The experimental design matrix comprised 17 runs, outlined in Table S4. Comprehensive results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the F-test are provided in Table S5, confirming the significance of pH, T, and C0. The effective visualization of the interaction between pairs of factors is depicted in Fig. 3a-c.

The impact of pH on the adsorption of DOX by LCCH-3 was investigated. The Qₑ of LCCH-3 increased from pH 4 to pH 6, then gradually decreased from pH 6 to pH 8, with a peak value of 17.25 mg/g at pH 6 (Fig. 3d). Zeta potential measurements revealed an isoelectric point of approximately 6 for LCCH-3 (Fig. S5). DOX exists in four forms: cationic (DOX+), anionic (DOX⁻ or DOX2⁻), and neutral (DOX0). DOX predominantly exists as DOX+ at pH levels below 3.5, as DOX0 between 3.5 and 7.1, and as DOX⁻ or DOX2⁻ above 7.1 (Wang et al., 2021). Under acidic conditions (pH < 6), the concentration of DOX⁺ increases as pH decreases (Kaur et al., 2021). Electrostatic repulsion between LCCH-3 and DOX⁺ decreased the Qₑ of LCCH-3. At pH 6, electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions between LCCH-3 and DOX0 led to the maximum Qₑ (Wei et al., 2019). Under alkaline conditions (pH > 7), deprotonation of LCCH-3's active sites caused electrostatic repulsion towards DOX⁻, reducing the Qₑ of LCCH-3. Therefore, pH 6 was chosen for subsequent experiments.

Temperature significantly impacts the adsorption properties of LCCH-3 (Fig. 3e). The Qₑ of LCCH-3 for DOX increased with rising T from 20 °C to 60 °C, indicating that the DOX adsorption process was endothermic (Olusegun and Mohallem, 2019). For practical applications, subsequent experiments were conducted at 30 °C. Thermodynamic parameters including ΔG, ΔH, and ΔS, were calculated using the temperature-dependent sorption and Van’t Hoff equations, as illustrated in Fig. 3e. The relevant equations are provided in the Supplementary Material. Negative ΔG° values indicated that DOX adsorption was thermodynamically spontaneous and feasible (Sharma et al., 2022). Furthermore, the low ΔH value of 19.52 kJ/mol suggested the endothermic nature of the adsorption process. The positive ΔS value (70.19 J/mol/K) indicated the increased randomness during the adsorption process (Tan and Zhou, 2024).

Fig. 3f illustrates the DOX adsorption capacities of LH, LCH, and LCCH-3 with respect to C0. Notably, LCCH-3 demonstrated a higher Qₑ than both LH and LCH at the same C0, due to the incorporation of CNs and CDs, which enhanced the adsorption process (Fan et al., 2019). The fitting curves using four adsorption isotherm models are presented in Figs. 3h, 3i, S6 and S7, respectively, with the fitting parameters detailed in Table S6. During the DOX adsorption process, the R2 value (0.9306) obtained from the Langmuir model exceeded those from the Freundlich model (0.8869), Temkin model (0.8848) and Dubinin-Radushkevich model (D-R model, 0.8771), indicating the superior explanatory power of the Langmuir model regarding the adsorption behavior of LCCH-3 (Stromer et al., 2018). Consequently, the computed Qm was 1686 mg/g, closely matching the actual result (Qₑ=1630 mg/g, C0=4000 mg/L). Compared with previously developed adsorbents ( Table 1), LCCH-3 exhibited a remarkable adsorption capacity for DOX.

Fig. 3g depicts the adsorption kinetics of DOX by LH, LCH, and LCCH-3 at a C0 of 60 mg/L. The variation of Qt showed that the adsorption rate of DOX was initially high, then slowed over time, and eventually plateaued at saturation. LCCH-3 consistently exhibited a larger Qt than LH and LCH, indicating the enhanced DOX adsorption facilitated by CNs and CDs. Fig. 3j and 3k illustrate the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics of LCCH-3 for DOX, with detailed data provided in Table S7. The R² value obtained from the pseudo-second-order kinetics (0.9916 in Fig. 3k) markedly surpassed that of the pseudo-first-order kinetics (0.9379 in Fig. 3j), suggesting a chemical reaction mechanism during the adsorption process (Sun et al., 2019).

The reusability of the adsorbent is crucial. LCCH-3 underwent five consecutive adsorption–desorption cycles to assess its reusability. After five cycles, LCCH-3 retained 80.4 % of its original adsorption capacity ( Fig. 4a), affirming its reusability and stability (Olusegun and Mohallem, 2019). SEM images (Fig. S8) depict negligible changes in the micromorphology of LCCH-3 before and after DOX adsorption, indicating the preservation of its 3D network structure throughout the adsorption process (Momina et al., 2020). Additionally, FTIR and XRD patterns (Fig. 4b and 4c) before and after the reaction reveal no appearance of new peaks post-cycle experiment, albeit a slight decrease in peak intensity, suggesting the preservation of LCCH-3's chemical structure (Wang et al., 2021). These findings collectively demonstrated the adsorption stability of LCCH-3.

To assess the practical utility of LCCH-3, DOX adsorption in real water was conducted. Three water samples were collected and their water quality parameters are listed in Table S8. Despite a slight reduction in Qe from tap water to lake water (as depicted in Fig. S9), LCCH-3 exhibited notable adsorption capacity across different water types, underscoring its potential for real-world applications.

LCCH-3 was utilized for antibiotic detection, demonstrating high selectivity for DOX, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (Ding et al., 2022). Upon the addition of DOX, noticeable fluorescence quenching occurred in LCCH-3, unaffected by the presence of other antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), ofloxacin (OFX), novobiocin (NOV), minocycline (MINO), enrofloxacin (ENR), chlortetracycline (CTC), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and chloramphenicol (CAP) (Fig. 5a). The molecular structures of these antibiotics are shown in Fig. S10. Additionally, Fig. 5b depicts a substantial alteration in the AQY/AQY0 of LCCH-3 upon DOX addition, further confirming its strong selectivity for DOX (Deng et al., 2023).

The optimization of DOX detection using LCCH-3 was conducted. As shown in Fig. S11a, the optimal pH for DOX detection is around 6. Additionally, Fig. S11b illustrates a rapid response time of LCCH-3 to DOX, within 8 min. The AQY of LCCH-3, as shown in Fig. S11c, remained consistent within the temperature range of 20–60 °C, with 30 °C selected for experimental convenience. Therefore, based on this analysis, DOX detection using LCCH-3 should be performed at pH 6, 30 °C, for 8 min.

Fig. 6a illustrates a gradual decrease in LCCH-3 fluorescence with increasing DOX concentration. The linear relationship between DOX concentration (10–120 mg/L) and AQY was determined by the equation: AQY = −0.0314×C0 + 10.71, with an R2 value of 0.9997. Additionally, the LOD was found to be 0.36 mg/L. Meanwhile, fluorescence intensities of LCCH-3 gradually quenched with rising DOX concentration (Fig. 6b), displaying a linear regression equation in the range of 0.2–1.0 μg/L: F0/F-1 = 0.2408×C0 + 0.2135 (R² = 0.9894), where F and F0 represented fluorescence intensities of LCCH-3 with and without DOX, respectively. The LOD for this correlation was calculated to be 0.08 μg/L (Kim et al., 2023). Furthermore, LCCH-3 demonstrated a broad linear range and enhanced sensitivity compared to most fluorescence-based methods for DOX detection, as depicted in Table 2.

The fluorescence detection of DOX by LCCH-3 was validated using real water samples, as presented in Table S9. Analysis of three water samples revealed no detection of DOX. The recoveries of spiked samples ranged from 95.0 % to 103.8 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 4.0 %. These findings suggest that LCCH-3 demonstrated robust detection capability for DOX.

The mechanism of DOX adsorption by LCCH-3 was investigated through FT-IR analysis conducted before and after adsorption. In Fig. 7a, the O−H stretching vibration of LCCH-3 shifted from 3310 cm−1 to 3290 cm−1 after DOX adsorption, accompanied by a decrease in intensity (Olusegun and Mohallem, 2019). This shift suggested hydrogen bonding formation between the O–H groups in LCCH-3 and nitrogen moieties in DOX (Kong et al., 2019). Additionally, a CO bond appeared at 1610 cm−1, indicating a π-π interaction between LCCH-3 and the aromatic ring of DOX (Olusegun and Mohallem, 2020).

To investigate the adsorption mechanism, XPS characterization of LCCH-3 before and after DOX adsorption was conducted. The XPS spectra in Fig. 7b revealed an increased N1s peak, indicating successful DOX adsorption onto LCCH-3 (Dieu et al., 2019). In Fig. 7c, two peaks were observed: C-O at 531.7 eV and CO at 532.4 eV (Han et al., 2021). Post-adsorption, these peaks shifted to lower binding energies, suggesting hydrogen bonding between DOX and LCCH-3 (Kong et al., 2019). Fig. 7d depicted the N1s spectrum, decomposed into three peaks at 398.7 eV, 400.2 eV, and 402.6 eV, corresponding to –N=, –NH2, and –NH3 + (Liu et al., 2024). Post-adsorption, the –NH2 peak shifted to 399.2 eV, indicating hydrogen bonding with DOX, while the –NH3 + peak shifted to 401.6 eV, suggesting electrostatic interactions (Chen et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2022). The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 7e) displayed peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.5 eV, and 286.0 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–O, and CO, respectively, with shifts to lower binding energies possibly due to π-π interactions (Kong et al., 2019). In conclusion, DOX interacted with LCCH-3 through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions.

To investigate the quenching mechanism of DOX on LCCH-3, we initially recorded the UV–vis absorption profile of DOX and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of LCCH-3. Fig. 8a reveals a UV absorption band at 345 nm for DOX, coinciding with fluorescence excitation peak of LCCH-3. This finding confirmed that the quenching mechanism of LCCH-3 was due to an internal filtration effect (IFE) (Qian et al., 2019). Subsequently, we analyzed the fluorescence lifetimes of LCCH-3 in the presence and absence of DOX (Fig. 8b-8d). It was found that the fluorescence lifetime curves of LCCH-3 with and without DOX remained essentially unchanged, with average lifetimes of 3.083 ns and 2.999 ns, respectively. Therefore, the DOX-induced fluorescence quenching of LCCH-3 was determined to be static quenching.

The proposed adsorption and detection mechanism of LCCH is depicted in Fig. 9. The 3D porous structured LCCH comprises lignin, CNs, and CDs. Lignin contains numerous active functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and aromatic groups, which adsorb DOX through hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions (Kong et al., 2019). CNs possess active functional groups like hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, facilitating DOX adsorption through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attractions (Soliman et al., 2023, Wu et al., 2022). CDs feature numerous amino groups that also adsorb DOX via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attractions. Additionally, the incorporation of CNs and CDs increases the surface area, thereby enhancing LCCH's adsorption capacity (Zhang et al., 2024). Furthermore, integrating CDs into the 3D porous structure of LCCH improves the stability of CDs by preventing self-quenching, enabling LCCH to function as a fluorescent probe with high accuracy for DOX detection.

Cost analysis studies are essential for estimating the price of LCCH-3, a critical parameter for comparison with other adsorbent materials. Using established cost-estimation methodologies (Kaur et al., 2021), the production costs for 1 kg of LCCH-3 were detailed in Table S10. The estimated price of LCCH-3 was CNY 20.81, representing only 8.67 % of the cost of activated carbon (CNY 240.00). Moreover, given its high adsorption capacity (Qm = 1686 mg/g as obtained in Section 3.3.4), the cost for treating 1 g of DOX with LCCH-3 was merely CNY 0.01/g, compared to CNY 10.17/g for activated carbon (Table S11). These findings indicated that LCCH-3 was a cost-effective adsorbent.

This study introduces LCCH, a novel adsorbent for the efficient adsorption and determination of DOX. The adsorption process on LCCH fits well with both the pseudo-second-order and Langmuir models, demonstrating a maximum adsorption capacity of 1686 mg/g. Additionally, DOX determination via fluorescence quenching of LCCH shows good linearity in two ranges: 10–120 mg/L and 0.2–1.0 μg/L, with a LOD of 0.08 μg/L. XPS and FTIR analyses validate LCCH's adsorption mechanism, involving electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bond, and π-π interaction. Spectrum analysis confirms the determination mechanism, implicating IFE and static quenching. These superior characteristics of LCCH stem from its 3D porous structures, composed of lignin, CDs, and CNs, which enhance specific surface area, active sites, and stabilize fluorescence signals. Consequently, this research paves the way for developing high-performance lignin-based hydrogels for antibiotic removal, thereby facilitating the valorization of lignin.

Meng Liu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jiayu Ouyang: Software, Resources, Formal analysis. Xinmiao Qi: Investigation, Data curation. Xin Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Ping Jiang: Software. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision.
